related_questions.question: What should I expect in reunification therapy?
related_questions.snippet: Parents may need to learn how to communicate more effectively, how to manage their own emotions, or how to develop healthier co-parenting strategies. The therapist will work with both parents to create a co-parenting plan that prioritizes the child's best interests.
related_questions.title: What to Expect in the Process of Family Reunification Therapy

related_questions.question: What is the best therapy for parental alienation?
related_questions.snippet: Reunification therapy occurs between the therapist and the family. The focus is threefold: tempering the hostilities of the alienating parent; assuring an emotional and safe environment for the children with both parents and significant others; and repairing the damaged relationships with the children.
related_questions.title: The Psychosocial Treatment of Parental Alienation

related_questions.question: What is the most common goal of reunification family therapy?
related_questions.snippet: Reunification therapy has a clear, single goal: the re-establishment of connection between parent and child and the restoration of their roles within the family and/or between separated parental households.
related_questions.title: Reunification Therapy | Psychology Today

related_questions.question: Who to call if your child is out of control?

text_blocks.snippet: When your child is out of control, call emergency services (911) for immediate danger, contact a youth mental health line like Kids Help Phone (1-800-668-6868 in Canada) for free support, or reach out to local Child & Family Services or mental health intake clinics for non-emergencies and ongoing help, depending on your location. For specific behavioral challenges, consider specialized programs or parent training resources from organizations like the Child Mind Institute.

list.snippet: 911 or Local Police: For immediate danger or safety concerns.
Kids Help Phone (Canada): Call 1-800-668-6868 or text CONNECT to 686868 for 24/7 confidential support for youth and parents.
Local Emergency Services: Check your local government's website for child welfare or mental health crisis lines.
Child & Family Services (CFS): Contact your regional CFS agency for reporting abuse/neglect or seeking family support.
Mental Health Intake Clinics: Search for Child & Youth Mental Health Intake Clinics in your province (e.g., BC's list available on their website) for local services.
Venture Academy: Offers specialized programs for youth with behavioral and mental health issues in Canada.
Child Mind Institute: Provides guides and resources on managing problem behaviors, anger, and finding parent training programs.
Children's Mental Health Ontario (CMHO): Connects parents with resources through organizations like Parents and Caregivers for Mental Health.
